author: nero_eth
content:
+1, let's make the timeout shorter.
The 1s timeout has been particularly stressful for min-bid users. They wait 1s for the bid while stopping iterating on the locally built block at 0s, wasting 1s for nothing.
Since timeouts are sent to the relay, sophisticated proposer can increase that value, making the relay delay the response, but at least they can't for "honest" proposers who just want the response immediately.

author: tersec
content: In which case, see the ping times linked. I'm, again, not claiming they're necessarily authoritative, accurate, or true. But just deciding to aggressively go for 200ms (or even 300ms) is questionable

author: tersec
content: Whatever the ping times from/to Auckland (or other similar places) are, they should not but cut off from this API for the sake of aggressive timeouts

author: tersec
content: And <@755590043632140352> handwaved 200ms to start with, so it's not as if that number has salient provenance, he just wants it fast

author: m.kalinin
content:
I have a question on testing related to ePBS.

This PR: https://github.com/ethereum/consensus-specs/pull/5449 touches payload generation for the genesis block and as far as i can get from the existing design, there was a convention to leave genesis block an empty block and make its bid refer to a "genesis" payload. In other words, genesis block is empty but it is "built" on top of a payload that is kind of "genesis" payload. The PR doesn't change this behavior, it just changes the `parent_block_hash` in the genesis block bid.

I want to confirm if my understanding on how to properly handle genesis block payload in tests is correct. cc <@586161934425128960> <@755590043632140352>

author: nero_eth
content: yeah not saying to go for 200ms. I'd keep it rather conservative. Maybe 400 or 500ms
